result:
There is a clock in the hallway of the boarding house where I grew up, and it ticks louder than time. I hear it even now, long after the railway lines have absorbed the last of my heartbeat. Trains used to rattle along those tracks at dusk, flashing red signals like stamped passports under dull sodium light. Every departure felt like a rehearsal for another absence, as if the metal wheels had learned the script beforehand and kept reciting it faithfully across rusting rails. I wonder sometimes if anyone has oiled the pulse of that clock since I left, just as I wonder if the tracks still sing metal songs to children who press ears to the ground, collecting shivers the way investors collect coins.

I was one of those children. I thought sound was a type of currency; anything loud enough might buy me answers. After my mother disappeared—ascended might be closer to the truth, she bent toward the windowpane like a candle finally tasting its own wax—I flattened coins on the rails so they might flatten me back. One day the steel pressed Abraham Lincoln into an ellipsis. The penny became infinite, edgeless, soft as guilt.

A man who claimed to be my father once said that memory is a ledger someone else keeps for you. He kept his in amber prescription bottles, shaken nightly, small white pills clicking like distant carriages. He was wrong. Memory is a game of tick-tack-toe played inside a burning paper lantern. Every X and O lifts off as ash before you know who wins, but the heat stays on your fingertips. I carry that heat everywhere. I passed it to the girl on the night train to Zagreb who traded her secrets for warm walnuts in my pockets. I passed it to the skinny sculptor in Santiago who molded my left ear in clay but forgot its twin, so my bronze head forever listens only in mono. I keep trying to give it away, this smoldering souvenir, but it reproduces faster than dusk in December.

During my tenth winter the hallway clock stopped at 8:17, a moment I still carry inside like a swallowed marble. Everything that happened afterward feels legible only through that frozen face. I tell people 8:17 is the hour when possibility shed its last crystal wing. They smile with polite disbelief, the way tourists smile at intern guides who swear the stone lions open their mouths when no one looks. I believe the lions. I believe every abandoned chair still holds the temperature of the last body it cradled. I believe the orchard behind my childhood house bears fruit only the blind can taste, peaches with thin skins of wind and pulp of almost. Each December I sneak back to steal one for my tongue, return to the city pretending I have eaten proof of something. Proof is heavier than memory and lighter than longing; it ripples in me like piano strings humming after the lid is shut.

I once taught English in a port town where the clouds resembled arithmetic flung across sky. Students asked why English says “once upon a time” instead of “one time upon.” I told them fairytales lie horizontally, they need horses to gallop across width, not climbable height. I didn’t tell them that I have lived my entire life three inches above the floor, skimming like a skipped stone that forgot how to sink. I wrote the sentence “We ache in the languages we do not speak” on the blackboard and then could not pronounce ache aloud, because the word suddenly looked like a small wooden chair with one leg broken.

When language fails, bodies invent new grammars. A baker in Lyon touched my wrist with floury fingertips to propose marriage, spelling yes-no-maybe in burnt sugar. I inhaled the smoke and answered maybe, leaving with only a paper bag warm against my coat. Under later stars I understood I had inhaled his family, too; the ghosts of grandfathers wearing aprons, kneading grief into dough that never rose. Still the smell of scalded milk makes my collarbones tender, because tenderness itself is a language we forget and relearn like contact lists after each distant war.

At twenty-nine I arrived in a city that had no echo. Tall glass rectangles threw my voice back as shards. When I called hello they answered hel-l-o-o- the walls sliced consonants, returned them unusable and pitiful. But within that city was a café tiled blue like the inside of a throat. The barista, whose eyelashes carried more weight than most histories, offered me a drink called mimosis. She explained: it was a curtain of champagne and powdered flower stamens; one tasted spring’s attempt at condolences. I drank three. Each sip painted my tongue the bright yellow of caution tape and bruised daffodil. I walked back into the echo-less streets hearing chlorophyll crack inside my veins. I mispronounced every streetlight; they forgave me by blinking slower till I could cross.

Somewhere along the atlas I traded my passport for a lighter and the lighter for directions to a river. The river had no name I could pronounce, so I addressed it as “Esteemed Listener.” In return it taught me how to unwrap darkness like a boiled sweet, slowly enough that the sugar does not shatter. I practiced nightly until I could hold the whole night inside a single lungful, carry it through encampments of fireflies that spelled Morse dreams above the grass. When I exhaled at dawn, small wisps of it crawled toward the horizon like shy kittens. Some mornings I find them in my coffee, purring.

The truth is I still don’t know whether I am collecting departures or arrivals, whether the difference matters any more than the two hackberry seeds lodged in a seam of my old suitcase. I cling to tiny immovable anchors: the chipped blue cup from which my mother once drank rationed sunrise, the ticket stub for a film I fell asleep inside, the cricket that sang inside a matchbox for three hours before choosing freedom. These objects speak to one another in hushed frequencies, ancient as glacier grammar. Together they form an archive whose index lives inside my wrist, beating arrhythmically whenever I pass a station whose benches gleam like wet ravens.

I want to tell the children pressing ears to rails that time is impatient and evasive, but also porous and edible. I want to assure them that every goodbye secretly waves a second hand from the pocket of its coat, waiting to be recognized. Most of all I want to place the hallway clock gently on the tracks, let the 8:17 moment shatter into train wheels that run both directions at once. Let pieces leap up like startled birds, scattering bronze feathers of almost and forever across miles that never agreed to be measured.

If you find one on your windowsill tomorrow morning, do what you will with it. Plant it in damp soil. Slip it under a stranger’s pillow. Swallow it with mimosis. Whatever you choose, remember: the heat stays on your fingertips.
